papers.title: Can supernova from runaway stars mimic the signs of absorbing `super-virial' gas?
papers.author_names: Mukesh Singh Bisht Projjwal Banerjee Biman B. Nath Yuri Shchekinov

abstract.text: The recent detection of large column density absorption lines from highly ionized gas in a few directions through the circumgalactic medium (CGM) of the Milky Way (MW) has been puzzling. The inferred temperature from these absorption lines far exceeds the virial temperature of the MW, and the column densities are also too large to be easily explained. In this paper, we propose a novel idea to explain these observations and claim that they may not have originated from the CGM, but from a totally different type of source, namely, stellar ejecta from supernovae (SNe) above the Galactic disk that happen to lie in the line of sight to the background quasars. About $\\\\sim 20\\\\%$ of massive OB stars (progenitors of core-collapse supernovae) are known to be runaway stars that have high ejection velocities near the Galactic plane and can end up exploding as SNe above the Galactic disk. We show that the associated reverse shock in the supernova remnant in the early non-radiative phase can heat the ejecta to temperatures of $\\\\gtrsim 10^7\\\\,{\\\\rm K}$ and can naturally explain the observed high column density of ions in the observed `super-virial' phase along with $\\\\alpha$-enriched super-solar abundance that is typical of core-collapse supernovae. However, SNe from runaway stars has a covering fraction of $\\\\lesssim 0.7 \\\\%$ and thus can only explain the observations along limited sightlines.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper challenges the conventional interpretation of 'super-virial' gas in the Milky Way's circumgalactic medium (CGM) by proposing that high-temperature, α-enhanced absorption lines instead originate from reverse shocks in supernova remnants of runaway massive stars. These stars, ejected above the Galactic plane with high velocities, produce ejecta heated to ≥10⁷ K during the early non-radiative phase of their supernova explosions, naturally explaining both the extreme column densities and α-enrichment observed in absorption. The model predicts such features only along a small fraction (∼0.7%) of sightlines due to the low covering fraction of runaway SN remnants, which aligns with the limited number of observed super-virial phases.

source_fields.title: Can supernova from runaway stars mimic the signs of absorbing `super-virial' gas?

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The reverse shock propagates inward, causing ejecta compression and heating, and eventually leads to an implosion at $t^* \\gtrsim 1$ followed by a bounce at the center or core–envelope boundary ($t^* \\gtrsim 2$), resulting in a secondary blast wave that propagates outward, with core presence significantly slowing the reverse shock and altering the shock dynamics, as evidenced by pressure, density, and mean molecular weight profiles across different $\\beta$ values. Summary: The observed high column densities of H-like ions in the Milky Way's circumgalactic medium, indicating super-virial temperatures, are naturally explained by reverse shock heating of metal-enriched ejecta from supernovae of runaway stars, with such features likely coincidental along specific lines of sight. Discussion: The observed column densities of OVIII, NeX, and SiXIV are naturally explained by supernovae in the extra-planar region of the Milky Way occurring 100–400 kyr after explosion, with models showing consistent agreement between predicted and observed ion column densities across three lines of sight, and runaway OB star progenitors accounting for approximately 1000 such events within a 250-kyr window, supported by Galactic dynamics simulations. Introduction: This paper makes a novel contribution by challenging the assumption of collisional ionization equilibrium (CIE) in the super-virial phase of the circumgalactic medium, instead inferring its existence and properties directly from observed high column densities of H-like ions—without relying on CIE models—thereby addressing the lack of a robust, observationally grounded framework for understanding this extreme phase. Simulation Of Core-Collapse Supernova And Evolution Of The Remnant: This section describes 1D simulations of massive star evolution, core-collapse supernovae, and the subsequent evolution of supernova remnants, incorporating detailed nucleosynthesis, mass loss, and hydrodynamic expansion under spherical symmetry.

title: Can supernova from runaway stars mimic the signs of absorbing `super-virial' gas?

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper proposing runaway star SNe as explanation for absorption features; no observational stellar data presented
